title: WAICA, the WAIC Academic conference, opens in Shanghai with Yao and Sutton at the head
subtitle: The inaugural peer-reviewed track of the World AI Conference draws 284 papers from 11 countries and nine Nobel and Turing laureates.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: WAICA — the World AI Conference · Academic — opened in Shanghai on July 18, 2026 as the first peer-reviewed academic track ever hosted under the WAIC umbrella. Andrew Chi-chih Yao, the 2000 Turing Award laureate and Tsinghua professor, chairs the conference; Richard Sutton, the reinforcement-learning pioneer at the University of Alberta, is international co-chair. Organizers report 284 valid paper submissions from 11 countries including Princeton, Cambridge and Tsinghua, with accepted papers to be published by Springer. Nine Nobel and Turing laureates are scheduled to participate — a lineup that includes Yoshua Bengio and Nobel economist Thomas J. Sargent.

This is a distinct event from the WAIC political conference and the WAICO governance alliance that Xi Jinping launched on July 17. WAICA is a technical venue with reviewers, a paper program and Springer publication — the structural moves that turn a national showcase into a peer-recognized academic conference. The fact that Sutton agreed to co-chair signals that at least some of the Western AI research community is willing to engage on the technical track, even while sitting out the governance one.

The choice of Sutton in particular is thematically loaded. His 'bitter lesson' essay is one of the most-cited pieces of writing in modern machine learning: methods that leverage more computation win over methods that hand-craft human knowledge. Having Sutton co-chair a Chinese-hosted conference during a week when China is announcing 8,192-chip SuperPoDs, 2.8-trillion-parameter open models and a 29-country AI governance bloc reads less like a coincidence and more like an editorial statement about where the compute is going.

For learners and teachers: watch what WAICA actually publishes. A national academic conference stands or falls on the strength of its papers, not its ribbon-cutters. If the accepted work turns out to be genuinely competitive with NeurIPS and ICML — especially in reinforcement learning, systems, and safety — the geographic center of AI research publishing will have shifted meaningfully, and reading Chinese-hosted proceedings will move from optional to necessary for anyone serious about the field.
